title: ‘Dazed and Confused’: Making a Modern Movie Classic, With Richard Linklater
format: Sean and Amanda each rank their five favorite Richard Linklater films, then Sean talks to the director and 'Alright, Alright, Alright' author Melissa Maerz about how Dazed and Confused got made.

segments.heading: The case for Richard Linklater
segments.note: Sean and Amanda on the filmography

films.title: Slacker
films.note: His first feature, made 30 years ago. Sean calls it one of the most important American independent movies ever made: non-linear, plotless, a tapestry of Austin voices that adds up to a thesis statement about form.

films.title: The Newton Boys
films.note: A late-'90s crime-comedy with McConaughey, Ethan Hawke, and Skeet Ulrich. Sean's example of Linklater trying something mainstream in his own style, off both hosts' lists.

films.title: School of Rock
films.note: The more successful version of that mainstream swing. Neither host puts it in a top five; Amanda notes the Jack Black online fandom she'd rather not provoke.

films.title: Fast Food Nation
films.note: The issues-oriented drama Sean says didn't land as well, cited as proof of how far Linklater will stretch tone and genre.

films.title: Waking Life
films.note: The rotoscoped, openly philosophical film Sean holds up as Linklater building an elaborate form around emotionally direct material.

films.title: Dazed and Confused
films.note: No. 1. Sean calls it a perfect movie he never doesn't want to watch, ahead of even The Godfather or Jaws, and thinks that's down to when he first saw it.

films.title: Before Sunset
films.note: No. 2. He takes Sunset over Sunrise because it feels real: a broken Hawke at a crossroads, the car conversation, the final scene where Delpy sings and the baby's going to miss the plane.

films.title: Everybody Wants Some!!
films.note: No. 3 for Sean, No. 5 for Amanda. The spiritual sequel to Dazed; a movie about male chemistry and the pleasure of a good hang, bungled by the studio and now slowly finding its cult.

films.title: A Scanner Darkly
films.note: No. 4. The rotoscoped Philip K. Dick adaptation with Keanu Reeves and Robert Downey Jr., Sean's stand-in for Linklater's willingness to take a hard left turn and not fear failing.

films.title: Boyhood
films.note: No. 5. Shot over 12 years with Ellar Coltrane and an Oscar-winning Patricia Arquette. Sean was crying on the rewatch; he's less sure it's a great film than a special accomplishment.

films.title: Before Sunrise
films.note: No. 1. She's a Sunrise person: it stands on its own, the two leads are on the most even footing, and it captures the goofy, overly verbal hope of falling in love.

films.title: Dazed and Confused
films.note: No. 2. A perfect film that replaced history for her even without any nostalgia for the '70s or high school, which is why it lands at two instead of one.

films.title: Before Sunset
films.note: No. 3. Written with Hawke and Delpy, it carries their real lives into it; she calls the trilogy some of the best portrayals of falling in and out of love ever made.

films.title: Before Midnight
films.note: No. 4. The fight scene gets at how couples actually fight; she calls the film a horror movie. It's fourth only because she's aware of the writing, so it plays more like a play.

films.title: Everybody Wants Some!!
films.note: No. 5. His most honest and, for her, most pleasurable movie: charming athletes who just want to hang out. She wonders if it'd be a Netflix top 10 hit today.

films.title: Bernie
films.note: The Jack Black and Matthew McConaughey movie Sean rates among Linklater's best.

films.title: Last Flag Flying
films.note: Underrated in Sean's view, a spiritual sequel to Hal Ashby's The Last Detail.

films.title: Where'd You Go, Bernadette
films.note: Sean's honorable mention: a fascinating experiment that doesn't quite come together, with a Cate Blanchett performance he likes a lot. The Bernadette character shuts herself off from the world, which he thinks is finally at odds with Linklater's outward-reaching people.

films.title: SubUrbia
films.note: The Eric Bogosian play he adapted, not a perfect movie, but a portrait of late-'90s indie cinema after Dazed and Before Sunrise.

films.title: Me and Orson Welles
films.note: About Welles running the Mercury Theater, made well before Mank, and an early Zac Efron role.

segments.heading: ‘Dazed and Confused’ in 2020
segments.note: Sean and Amanda on the centerpiece

films.title: Dazed and Confused
films.note: Both hosts call it a perfect film. Sean says it worked as a field guide for how to be a young person; Amanda, with no nostalgia for the '70s or high school, argues its craft (every cue and every actor right) makes it feel lived-in anyway. Both flag how little attention the female characters get, and single out Parker Posey's performance.

segments.heading: In conversation
segments.note: Richard Linklater and Melissa Maerz on the oral history

films.title: Dazed and Confused
films.note: Maerz's book 'Alright, Alright, Alright' started from Linklater calling it an intended anti-nostalgia movie that became the ultimate nostalgia movie. He walks through the early, much darker draft, the freshman hazing the town came out to watch, the studio treating it as an indie release after he'd gone through studio hell, and losing the Led Zeppelin song he wanted.

films.title: School of Rock
films.note: Ten years later, Linklater finally landed a Led Zeppelin song here, his 'redemptive cycle' after the fight over Dazed's soundtrack.
